question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: fileHash)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where fileHash and hash differ (since they are stored separately), exploiting any code path that uses fileHash for integrity checks while hash is used for chain linking, to inject a file with modified fee data that passes one check but not the other?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: items)] Since items is @Transient and @EqualsAndHashCode.Exclude, can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that two RecordFile instances with identical persistent fields but different items (containing different charged_tx_fee values) are considered equal, causing a cache or deduplication layer to serve stale fee data from a previously cached instance?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: items)] Since items is a mutable List<RecordItem> with a public setter (generated by @Data), can an unprivileged external user exploit a code path that exposes the RecordFile object to replace the items list with RecordItems containing zero or negative transactionFee values before the importer persists them?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: count)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where count is set to a value lower than the actual number of items, causing the importer to stop processing items early and skip fee-bearing transactions, resulting in incomplete fee records in the database?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: count)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where count is set to a value higher than the actual number of items, causing the importer to attempt to read beyond the available items and either throw an exception (DoS) or process garbage data as fee-bearing transactions?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: sidecarCount)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where sidecarCount is set to a value higher than the actual number of sidecars, causing the importer to expect additional sidecar files containing fee-related data (e.g., contract state changes affecting gas fees) that never arrive, leading to incomplete fee processing?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: sidecars)] Since sidecars is @Transient and @EqualsAndHashCode.Exclude, can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that sidecars are excluded from equality and persistence to inject sidecar records with manipulated gas_used values that affect EVM fee calculations without being detected by hash-based integrity checks on the RecordFile itself?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: logsBloom)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with a manipulated logsBloom byte array that causes the BackfillBlockMigration to compute an incorrect gasUsed total (via LogsBloomFilter.or()), leading to inflated or deflated block_gas_used values that distort EVM fee reporting?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: logsBloom)] Since logsBloom is a mutable byte[] with a public setter and is @ToString.Exclude (not logged), can an unprivileged external user silently replace the logsBloom value after file ingestion to corrupt the bloom filter used in gas/fee calculations without leaving a trace in logs?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: index)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with a manipulated index (block number) that causes fee-schedule lookups keyed on block number to retrieve an incorrect fee schedule, resulting in wrong fee estimates being served via the REST API?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: index)] Can an unprivileged external user supply a record file with index=null (since it is a nullable Long), causing block-number-based fee queries to fail or return null results, leading to a denial of service for fee estimation endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: digestAlgorithm)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with digestAlgorithm set to a weaker or unexpected algorithm value, causing hash verification to use a different algorithm than expected and allowing a file with manipulated fee data to pass integrity checks?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: metadataHash)] Since metadataHash is @Transient and @JsonIgnore, can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that it is not persisted or serialized to inject a record file where the metadataHash does not match the actual file content, bypassing metadata integrity checks and allowing fee-manipulated records to be accepted?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: softwareVersionMajor/Minor/Patch)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where softwareVersionMajor/Minor/Patch differs from hapiVersionMajor/Minor/Patch, exploiting any code path that uses softwareVersion instead of hapiVersion for fee-schedule selection to apply an incorrect (older) fee schedule?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: roundStart/roundEnd)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with roundStart/roundEnd values that fall outside the expected range for the given consensusStart/consensusEnd, causing round-based fee-schedule lookups to retrieve an incorrect fee schedule and report wrong fees?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: bytes)] Since bytes is a mutable byte[] with a public setter and @ToString.Exclude, can an unprivileged external user replace the raw bytes of a RecordFile after initial parsing but before hash verification, injecting modified transaction fee data that is stored in the database without detection?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: bytes)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where the bytes field contains a valid protobuf-encoded file with manipulated transactionFee values in the embedded TransactionRecord objects, causing the importer to parse and store incorrect charged_tx_fee values?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: name)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with a name that encodes a timestamp (e.g., 2019-08-30T18_10_00.419072Z.rcd) that predates the current fee schedule, causing the importer to associate the file with an older fee schedule based on the filename timestamp rather than the actual consensusStart?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: previousWrappedRecordBlockHash)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with a forged previousWrappedRecordBlockHash that matches a legitimate block's hash, breaking the block hash chain and allowing a file with manipulated fee data to be accepted as a valid block continuation?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: wrappedRecordBlockHash)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file with a manipulated wrappedRecordBlockHash that causes the block hash chain to be accepted as valid despite containing modified fee data in the embedded transactions?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: size)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record file where the size field is set to a value inconsistent with the actual bytes length, causing size-based validation or fee-per-byte calculations to produce incorrect results?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: loadStart/loadEnd)] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate loadStart or loadEnd timestamps to cause the importer to believe a file was loaded at a different time, affecting any time-based fee-schedule selection logic that uses load timestamps instead of consensus timestamps?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: hapiVersion()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a record file with hapiVersionMajor=Integer.MAX_VALUE, hapiVersionMinor=Integer.MAX_VALUE, hapiVersionPatch=Integer.MAX_VALUE, causing the Version comparison logic to overflow or behave unexpectedly and select an incorrect fee schedule?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: hapiVersion()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a record file with negative hapiVersionMajor/Minor/Patch values (since they are Integer, not validated for non-negativity), causing Version comparisons to produce unexpected results and select an incorrect fee schedule?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/transaction/RecordFile.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: RecordFile (field: items, @EqualsAndHashCode.Exclude)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that items is excluded from equals/hashCode to cause a HashMap or Set containing RecordFile objects to treat two files with different fee-bearing items as the same entry, leading to one file's fee data silently overwriting another's?
